migrationPlan.seed_strategy: Two-tier seed: (1) `019_seed_platform.sql` is reference/lookup data — pricing plans, addons, feature module catalog, 13 Saudi regions, major cities, named Saudi courts (General/Commercial/Labor/Personal Status/Administrative/Appeal/Supreme/Enforcement/Board of Grievances), law categories tree, and an idempotent UPSERT for the initial platform admin sourced from `INITIAL_PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAIL` env var via psql `\set`. This runs in ALL environments including production because the app cannot function without it. (2) `020_seed_dev_only.sql` is realistic fake firm data — guarded by a `DO $$ ... RAISE EXCEPTION IF current_database() NOT LIKE 'qaf_dev%' $$` block at top so production deploys abort if the file is ever included. Dev data: two demo tenants (`almuhami-demo`, `tahakum-demo`) with realistic Arabic names (e.g., عبدالله السبيعي, نورة القحطاني), valid-checksum Saudi national IDs, Riyadh/Jeddah addresses, 20-40 cases per tenant spanning all case types and statuses, linked hearings at seeded courts, SAR-denominated invoices with 15% VAT, time entries, requests with multi-step approvals, sample document metadata. One demo tenant on pro_199 plan, other on business_499 + bd_module addon — so devs can exercise feature-flag gating. CI seeds a transient `qaf_dev_ci` DB and runs RLS tests asserting tenant A cannot see tenant B's rows.
migrationPlan.dev_vs_prod: Environments share migrations 001-019 and diverge only at 020. Supabase CLI projects: `qaf-dev` (Frankfurt), `qaf-staging` (Frankfurt), `qaf-prod` (Frankfurt). Migration application: `supabase db push` against each linked project from CI; staging mirrors prod migrations one commit ahead. Production deploy pipeline explicitly excludes `020_seed_dev_only.sql` by renaming it out of `supabase/migrations/` into `supabase/seeds_dev/` — Supabase CLI only runs files inside `migrations/`. Belt-and-suspenders: the file's `current_database()` guard would also abort it. Production tenants are created exclusively via the signup flow, which writes to `platform.tenants` + bootstraps a tenant_membership for the registering user as `admin`. Production has zero seeded customer rows. Connection-string discipline: app uses two PG roles — `qaf_app` (NOLOGIN-via-PgBouncer, normal RLS subject, used for tenant requests after `SET LOCAL app.tenant_id`) and `qaf_platform` (used by admin.qaf.sa backend with `app.is_platform_admin=true`). The `postgres` superuser is only used by migrations. Feature flags differ per env via `platform.subscriptions` rows, not code branches. Local dev devs run `supabase start` for a local Postgres 15 then `supabase db reset` which replays 001-020.
migrationPlan.backup_strategy: Three layers. (1) Supabase PITR enabled on `qaf-prod` (Pro plan or higher) with 7-day retention — covers accidental DELETE / bad migration rollback to any second. (2) Nightly logical backup: GitHub Actions cron at 02:00 Riyadh runs `pg_dump --format=custom --no-owner --no-acl --schema=app --schema=platform --schema=audit --schema=public` against the pooled Supabase connection, encrypts with `age` using a public key whose private key is in 1Password, then `aws s3 cp` to `s3://qaf-backups-eu-central-1/prod/YYYY/MM/DD/qaf-prod-<ts>.dump.age`. Bucket has Object Lock (Compliance mode, 35-day retention) + versioning + lifecycle to Glacier Deep Archive after 30 days. EU residency preserved (eu-central-1 = Frankfurt, same region as Supabase project). (3) Weekly full backup + monthly restore drill: a separate workflow restores the latest dump into a throwaway `qaf-restore-test` Supabase project, runs schema-checksum + row-count smoke tests, reports to Slack. RPO target 24h, RTO 4h. Tenant-scoped "export my data" satisfies GDPR Article 20 by streaming the tenant's rows from every `app.*` table as JSONL — implemented as a SECURITY DEFINER function `platform.export_tenant(uuid)` callable only by platform admin or tenant owner. Hard delete for GDPR Article 17: `platform.purge_tenant(uuid)` runs in a transaction, deletes from all tables in FK-safe order, writes a final audit row, and deletes Storage objects under the `tenantid/` prefix — logged + irreversible.
migrationPlan.portability_notes: Pure-Postgres-first design so the schema can be lifted to self-hosted Postgres 15+ on the OVH VPS or any RDS-equivalent. Concrete choices: (1) Migrations live under `supabase/migrations/` but use only standard SQL — no `auth.users`, no `auth.uid()`, no `storage.*` table references inside RLS policies. Identity is held in `app.users` (we mirror Supabase auth into it via a thin sync trigger that lives in a SEPARATE file `099_supabase_auth_sync.sql` outside the portable core; on non-Supabase deploys that file is skipped and the app writes `app.users` directly). (2) RLS reads tenant/user/admin context from `current_setting('app.tenant_id', true)::uuid` etc., set by the app via `SET LOCAL` per transaction — works on any Postgres, no JWT-claim dependency. (3) Helper functions are plain PL/pgSQL `SECURITY DEFINER`; no `auth.jwt()`, no Supabase Vault. (4) Storage paths are stored in `app.documents.storage_bucket` + `storage_path`; the app abstracts the backend so Supabase Storage today, S3/MinIO later — switching providers is a config change. (5) Scheduling: prefer external scheduler (GitHub Actions / VPS cron) over `pg_cron` so the materialized view refresh and nightly jobs don't require a Supabase-only extension; `pg_cron` is used as an OPTIONAL optimization gated behind `CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_cron` inside a `DO` block that no-ops if unavailable. (6) Realtime / Edge Functions are treated as adapters, not core — the schema does not depend on `supabase_realtime` publications; if needed they're added in `098_supabase_realtime.sql` (also outside portable core). (7) UUIDs via `gen_random_uuid()` from `pgcrypto` (standard), not `uuid-ossp`. (8) Search uses `pg_trgm` + `unaccent` (standard contrib), not Supabase-only fts helpers. (9) Enums declared with explicit schema qualifiers so they survive a `pg_dump` + restore into a different project. (10) No use of `security definer` views that hide `auth.uid()` — all views are `SECURITY INVOKER` (Postgres 15 feature) so they re-evaluate RLS on the calling role. Result: a `pg_dump` of `app`, `platform`, `audit` schemas restores cleanly onto a vanilla Postgres 15+ instance; a one-page runbook documents the 2-3 Supabase-specific adapter files that need to be replaced when migrating off.

findings.where: public.users — policy users_same_tenant_select; ALL tenant tables using `tenant_id = (SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())`
findings.issue: Recursive RLS evaluation hazard and lookup-loop. RLS on public.users itself uses a subquery on public.users to resolve tenant_id, which can recurse and either fail-closed silently or be bypassed depending on Postgres planner. The design notes say JWT claims are populated via an auth hook to AVOID this recursive lookup, but the canonical RLS expression printed in rls_principles still hits public.users. Mixing both forms (subquery vs auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id') across tables means any forgotten table will silently degrade to a subquery that may return NULL for a freshly-signed-up user (no public.users row yet because trigger lag) and `NULL = NULL` is UNKNOWN — fail-closed for reads but may also allow INSERT with NULL tenant_id slipping through WITH CHECK if the WITH CHECK is written the same way.
findings.recommended_fix: Pick ONE source of truth — `(auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid` — and ban the public.users subquery form in CI (grep). Add NOT NULL + CHECK (tenant_id IS NOT NULL) on every tenant table and ensure WITH CHECK does `tenant_id = (auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid AND tenant_id IS NOT NULL`.

findings.where: Auth doc claims `tenant_id` and `qaf_role` come from a custom_access_token_hook reading public.tenant_users, but schema has NO public.tenant_users table — it has public.users with tenant_id + role columns. Tenant_users is referenced in invite_flow, edge_cases (one_active_membership index), revocation, and membership_version.
findings.issue: The schema and the auth design are out of sync. If the hook actually reads from a non-existent table (or from public.users instead), the entire one-human-one-tenant invariant (`UNIQUE INDEX one_active_membership ON tenant_users(user_id) WHERE status='active'`) does NOT EXIST in the schema, so a single auth.users row could be linked to multiple public.users rows in different tenants via separate INSERTs — the hook would then pick one arbitrarily and the user could switch tenants by manipulating ordering or by re-issuing the JWT.
findings.recommended_fix: Either (a) add public.tenant_users junction table as described and migrate role/tenant_id off public.users, OR (b) rewrite auth.qaf_access_token_hook to read public.users with a hard constraint `UNIQUE (id) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` AND add a trigger preventing INSERT of a second public.users row for the same auth.users.id. Currently public.users.id is the PK so 1:1 holds, but then `one_active_membership` and `membership_version` are fictional and the revocation story collapses.

findings.where: All tenant tables relying on `(auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid` or `(auth.jwt()->>'qaf_role')` for write authorization; access token TTL = 1h.
findings.issue: A revoked or role-demoted user keeps a valid access token for up to ~60 min. Edge case doc admits this and says high-risk tables 'use the live join even for reads', but no policy in the printed RLS shows this live join. Auditor demoted to lawyer can still read finance for an hour; revoked user can still write to cases. For financial/audit tables this is unacceptable.
findings.recommended_fix: Add a mandatory `AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM public.users u WHERE u.id = auth.uid() AND u.tenant_id = (auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid AND u.status='active' AND u.deleted_at IS NULL AND u.role = (auth.jwt()->>'qaf_role')::user_role)` guard on payments, invoices, wallet_transactions, audit_log, tenant_users, subscriptions, expenses. Shorten access token TTL to 5-10 min for these or move to a session_invalidations table polled by a fast SECURITY DEFINER helper `qaf_current_user()` STABLE-cached per statement.

findings.where: rls_principles addon clause uses `auth.jwt() ->> 'enabled_addons'` array, but `enabled_addons` is refreshed only on token issue (~1h).
findings.issue: When an addon expires or is cancelled mid-session, the user keeps access for up to 1h. Worse, if `enabled_addons` is NULL in the JWT (older token, hook failure, anon role), `'bd_module' = ANY (auth.jwt()->'enabled_addons'::text[])` becomes NULL — semantically false, but combined with OR clauses elsewhere this is a foot-gun. Also, the JWT claim is named `enabled_addons` in auth doc but the tenant_addons.feature_key is free-form text — a tenant who sets feature_key='bd_module ' (trailing space) or with different casing would silently fail open or closed depending on comparison.
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Use a SECURITY DEFINER STABLE function `qaf_has_addon(text)` that reads tenant_addons live with `(tenant_id, feature_key, status, expires_at)` index — sub-millisecond. (2) Enforce a CHECK constraint on tenant_addons.feature_key against an enum or a referenced lookup table. (3) Coalesce NULL JWT claim to empty array.

findings.where: All buckets (case-documents, contracts, memos, templates, licenses, internal-docs, avatars, tenant-branding, audit-exports); RLS check `(storage.foldername(name))[1]::uuid = (SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())`
findings.issue: Path-based isolation is forgeable at INSERT time if WITH CHECK only validates the first folder segment. A tenant uploading with name `<otherTenantId>/cases/.../evil.pdf` would fail SELECT but if their tenant_id is a prefix-collision (unlikely with uuid) or if storage policy is only applied to SELECT not INSERT/UPDATE, they can plant files in another tenant's space. Additionally, the `templates` bucket carves out a global tenant_id `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000` 'readable by all tenants' — this special-case is RLS-bypass-by-convention; any tenant can write to it if INSERT policy isn't role-gated. The avatars bucket comment says 'user can write only their own (user_id = auth.uid() check in path)' but this requires parsing path segments and casting — easy to get wrong (e.g., `(storage.foldername(name))[2]` could be `${user_id}.png` and the policy doesn't strip the extension).
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Add identical USING and WITH CHECK on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on storage.objects per bucket. (2) For platform/global templates, use a SEPARATE bucket `platform-templates` (public read or anon read) — don't sentinel-tenant inside a tenant bucket. (3) Enforce avatar path with `name = (SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id=auth.uid())::text || '/avatars/' || auth.uid()::text || '.' || regexp_replace(name, '^.*\.', '')` regex check. (4) Add a Postgres trigger on storage.objects that re-validates path against tenant_id from JWT (defense in depth).

findings.where: payments.subscription_id → subscriptions.id; payments.addon_id → tenant_addons.id; users → auth.users.id; subscriptions.plan_id → subscription_plans.id; audit_log.actor_id → users.id (ON DELETE SET NULL)
findings.issue: FK constraints DO NOT enforce that the referenced row belongs to the SAME tenant. Tenant A could (via a SQL injection, a buggy RPC, or service-role misuse) INSERT a payment row with `tenant_id = A` but `subscription_id` belonging to tenant B. Nothing in the FK or RLS WITH CHECK validates this. Same for audit_log.actor_id = a user from a different tenant. RLS prevents READING but a write path with elevated privilege could corrupt cross-tenant references that later become invisible (RLS hides them) but accumulate referential garbage and could be exposed via aggregate queries or service-role exports.
findings.recommended_fix: Add COMPOSITE FKs: `FOREIGN KEY (tenant_id, subscription_id) REFERENCES subscriptions(tenant_id, id)` after adding `UNIQUE (tenant_id, id)` on every tenant table. Or add CHECK constraints via triggers that validate same-tenant on insert. Specifically for payments, audit_log, wallet_transactions, case_timeline, memo_history, attachments — any table that FKs another tenant table.

findings.where: users.tenant_id FK `ON DELETE CASCADE` to tenants; users.id FK `ON DELETE CASCADE` to auth.users.
findings.issue: Deleting an auth.users row (e.g., GDPR erase via supabase.auth.admin.deleteUser) cascades to public.users which cascades to every FK that references users.id (created_by, actor_id with ON DELETE SET NULL, but assigned_to etc may be CASCADE). This can hard-delete case ownership history mid-tenant. Worse: deleting a tenant CASCADEs users which CASCADEs their auth.users.id? No — FK direction is users.id → auth.users.id so deleting tenants cascades to users (orphans auth.users — they retain login but lose tenant). A bug deleting a tenant row would silently kick all those users back to the /no-tenant page but their auth sessions remain — combined with the 1h JWT staleness, those JWTs still claim `tenant_id` for the gone tenant and would attempt writes against RLS guards that don't yet exist (tenant_status='deleted' is mentioned but not in enums — enum only has trialing/active/past_due/suspended/cancelled, no 'deleted').
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Change tenants ON DELETE to RESTRICT — never hard-delete a tenant; use deleted_at + cron. (2) Add 'deleted' to tenant_status enum or align doc to use 'cancelled'. (3) Audit FK actions across all tables — for actor_id, created_by, assigned_to use ON DELETE SET NULL or RESTRICT, never CASCADE.

findings.where: moyasar_webhook Edge Function runs as service_role; payments_platform_write policy permits ALL for platform_admins; webhook handler 'updates subscription status atomically'.
findings.issue: Webhook handler with service_role bypasses RLS entirely. A spoofed webhook (if HMAC verification fails open, has a timing bug, or signing secret leaks) could update ANY tenant's subscription status to 'active' or insert a fake 'paid' payment, granting free service or unlocking addons. The doc says 'validates Moyasar HMAC signature first' but doesn't specify constant-time comparison, replay protection (idempotency by moyasar_event_id), or signature scope (webhook-specific secret vs API key).
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Per-event idempotency: store moyasar_event_id as UNIQUE in a `webhook_events` table, reject duplicates. (2) Use crypto.timingSafeEqual for HMAC. (3) Service-role queries inside webhook must always filter `WHERE tenant_id = $resolved_from_moyasar_customer_id` — never trust webhook body's tenant_id directly. (4) Move webhook to its own least-privileged DB role with grants only on payments, subscriptions, tenant_addons — not full service_role.

findings.where: subscription_plans plans_public_read policy allows `roles=['authenticated','anon']` with USING true.
findings.issue: Plans table is intentionally public, which is fine for tier/price, but the `features` jsonb may include internal flags or absolute limits that reveal product roadmap. Anon also gets `is_active=false` rows (no filter).
findings.recommended_fix: Add `AND is_active = true` to the USING expression; project only public columns via a view `public_plans` and revoke anon SELECT on the base table.

findings.where: platform_admins_self policy: `user_id = auth.uid() OR EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM platform_admins pa WHERE pa.user_id = auth.uid())`
findings.issue: This policy on platform_admins references platform_admins inside itself — Postgres will recurse with RLS re-applied on the inner SELECT. Supabase docs explicitly warn about this. Result: either infinite recursion (error) or the inner check inherits the same outer policy and fails closed, breaking the `EXISTS ... platform_admins` guard used by EVERY platform table (tenants, subscriptions, payments, addons platform_write policies).
findings.recommended_fix: Wrap in SECURITY DEFINER function `is_platform_admin()` STABLE that runs with `SET LOCAL row_security = off` and read-only privileges on platform_admins. Use that function everywhere instead of inline EXISTS.

findings.where: rls_principles: 'SELECT policies on user-facing tables include AND deleted_at IS NULL except for admins/auditors'
findings.issue: Auditor role can read soft-deleted rows (intended for compliance) but the same exception applies to `admin` and `general_manager`. If a user-facing record is soft-deleted because it contained sensitive cross-tenant leakage or was withdrawn for GDPR reasons before the 30-day purge, admin/auditor can still surface it. More importantly, GDPR right-to-erasure says 30-day cron does hard delete — but auditor exports during that window will include data the data subject requested erased. Compliance gap.
findings.recommended_fix: Distinguish soft-delete reasons via `deletion_reason` enum (`user_action`, `gdpr_erasure`, `tenant_purge`). RLS exception applies only to `user_action`. GDPR-erased rows are invisible to all roles immediately.

findings.where: audit_log.changes jsonb (before/after); subscriptions.metadata; payments.raw_payload; tenants.settings; notifications.payload; users — no jsonb but role is column.
findings.issue: audit_log.changes stores full row snapshots of sensitive tables (cases, contracts, invoices, salaries, wallets) as jsonb. RLS on audit_log restricts SELECT by tenant_id + role, but the snapshot may contain encrypted PII or cross-references to other tenants' identifiers (case parties, opponent firms) that were never meant to be queryable. JSONB columns are not subject to column-level grants and a GIN index on audit_log.changes (mentioned in indexes_strategy) enables enumeration via path queries even for users with table SELECT — they can `WHERE changes @> '{"after":{"client_email":"x@y.com"}}'` and infer existence cross-time.
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Redact PII before writing to changes: replace email/phone/national_id with sha256 hash + tenant-scoped salt. (2) Drop the GIN index on audit_log.changes; force index access through structured columns. (3) For roles other than admin/auditor, project audit_log through a view that strips before/after snapshots and only shows action+entity_type+entity_id.

findings.where: subscription_plans, platform_admins, tenants itself have no tenant_id (correctly), but ensure NO tenant table accidentally lacks it.
findings.issue: Schema truncation cuts off many tables (cases, clients, memos, invoices, expenses, wallets, wallet_transactions, capacity_entries, consulting_hours, notifications, requests, tasks, schedule_events, attachments, contracts, licenses, leads, regulations, templates, memo_history, case_timeline, support tickets). I cannot verify each has tenant_id + RLS. Based on prior multi-tenant audits, the highest-risk omissions are: memo_history (append-only, sometimes inherits tenant only via memo FK), case_timeline (same), attachments (linking table often missed), notifications (often only has user_id), and any global reference tables (regulations) that mix global + tenant rows.
findings.recommended_fix: CI check: query information_schema for any table in public schema with RLS DISABLED or no policy, and any table without a tenant_id column except a strict allowlist (tenants, platform_admins, subscription_plans, lookup tables). Fail build on diff.

findings.where: Middleware compares URL subdomain → tenant_id with jwt.tenant_id, mismatch returns 404. Subdomain is citext UNIQUE on tenants.
findings.issue: 404-on-mismatch is good for cross-tenant attempts WHILE LOGGED IN, but tenants.subdomain is queryable by edge function pre-auth ('resolves tenant_id from Host header before queries'). If the resolver uses anon key + RLS, anon can probably SELECT tenants by subdomain (no RLS policy shown for anon on tenants — only authenticated/platform_admin). If it uses service_role, no RLS — but timing-based enumeration is possible: existing subdomain vs not existing returns different latency. This lets an attacker enumerate all قاف tenant slugs.
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Add a tiny `tenant_slugs` materialized view or KV-cache in Edge runtime; use constant-time lookup. (2) For unknown subdomains, redirect to marketing site with same latency as known. (3) Rate-limit subdomain lookups by IP.

findings.where: tenants_self_select with_check_expr='true'; users_same_tenant_select with_check_expr='true'; subscriptions_tenant_read with_check_expr='true'; addons_tenant_read with_check_expr='true'; payments_tenant_read with_check_expr='true'; plans_public_read with_check_expr='true'.
findings.issue: `with_check_expr: 'true'` on policies that include INSERT/UPDATE paths means any write satisfying the USING clause can write ANY tenant_id. For SELECT-only policies WITH CHECK is irrelevant, but if these are merged into ALL or if a future migration adds INSERT to the same policy, the literal `true` becomes a tenant-isolation hole. The platform_write policies correctly mirror USING into WITH CHECK; the *_tenant_read policies should explicitly limit operation to SELECT.
findings.recommended_fix: Make operation strictly SELECT on read policies (the schema says SELECT but `with_check_expr` should be NULL/omitted, not 'true'). Audit migration tool to forbid `WITH CHECK (true)` outside explicit allowlist.

findings.where: audit_log, wallet_transactions, case_timeline, memo_history described as append-only — 'no UPDATE or DELETE policy granted'.
findings.issue: 'No policy' means denied for authenticated, but service_role bypasses RLS. A buggy Edge Function or cron with service_role can UPDATE/DELETE audit_log silently. Compliance impact: audit log is supposed to be tamper-evident.
findings.recommended_fix: (1) REVOKE UPDATE, DELETE on these tables from service_role explicitly and create a dedicated role `qaf_writer` that has only INSERT, used by Edge Functions. (2) Add a trigger BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE that RAISES regardless of role (the only way to bypass is ALTER TABLE … DISABLE TRIGGER which requires superuser). (3) Stream audit_log to an external WORM store (Frankfurt S3 Object Lock) nightly.

findings.where: Auth doc says 'sensitive RLS policies include AND (auth.jwt()->>"aal") = aal2' but no policy printed shows this clause.
findings.issue: If only middleware enforces aal2 redirect, an attacker with a valid aal1 JWT calling PostgREST directly (curl with the token) bypasses MFA for any table whose policy lacks the aal check — including payments, audit_log, subscriptions, users (admin role changes). Defense-in-depth claim is unmet.
findings.recommended_fix: Add `AND (auth.jwt()->>'aal') = 'aal2'` to USING and WITH CHECK on: payments (any op), subscriptions (any op), tenant_addons (write), users (UPDATE role), audit_log (SELECT for non-auditor), wallet_transactions (write), and any RPC marked SECURITY DEFINER that mutates these — enforce via a guard `assert_aal2()` at function top.

findings.where: custom_access_token_hook qaf_access_token_hook returns tenant_id from public.users / tenant_users.
findings.issue: If the hook throws (NPE, missing row, transient DB error), Supabase Auth's behavior depends on configuration: either the token is issued WITHOUT custom claims (fail-open) or login fails. If fail-open and the JWT contains no `tenant_id`, then `(auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid` is NULL, and `tenant_id = NULL` is UNKNOWN → no rows visible (safe), BUT WITH CHECK `tenant_id = NULL` allows nothing only if tenant_id column is NOT NULL — which is the non-negotiable, so OK. However, if any policy uses `OR` (e.g., users_same_tenant_select `OR id = auth.uid()`), a hook-failure user with id = auth.uid() can read THEIR OWN public.users row — fine, but then app code may treat that row's tenant_id as authoritative and re-authenticate, ignoring the missing claim. The mismatch between 'JWT claim' approach and 'public.users row' approach for the SAME data is the root risk.
findings.recommended_fix: (1) Make the hook fail-CLOSED — if any error, deny login. Document and test this. (2) Pick claim OR subquery, not both. (3) Add a startup self-test in CI that signs in a test user and asserts JWT contains tenant_id, role, plan, enabled_addons keys.

findings.where: users_same_tenant_select USING: `tenant_id = (...) OR id = auth.uid()`
findings.issue: The `OR id = auth.uid()` lets a user read their own row even when their tenant subquery is NULL (e.g., between auth.users INSERT trigger and public.users INSERT). Good for self-fetch, but a platform_admin (tenant_id NULL) sees their own row plus any other user where `tenant_id = NULL`. If there are multiple platform_admins, they can list each other — likely intentional, but also any orphaned user (deleted_at not null with tenant_id nulled by cascade) becomes visible to all NULL-tenant users.
findings.recommended_fix: Tighten to: `tenant_id IS NOT NULL AND tenant_id = (auth.jwt()->>'tenant_id')::uuid OR (id = auth.uid() AND deleted_at IS NULL)`. Platform admin access to users goes through a dedicated `platform_admin_user_view` view, not the base table.

findings.where: `(storage.foldername(name))[1]::uuid = ...`
findings.issue: `::uuid` cast on a non-uuid first segment RAISES an error rather than returning false — making the policy evaluation throw. Supabase RLS treats throw as deny, so safe, but it (a) leaks existence by error message timing/text via API, (b) breaks platform-templates path `platform/{filename}` because 'platform' is not a uuid cast → error, not graceful skip.
findings.recommended_fix: Use `(storage.foldername(name))[1] = (SELECT tenant_id::text FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())` — text compare, no cast. For platform bucket, use a separate policy with `(storage.foldername(name))[1] = 'platform' AND <platform_admin_check>`.

findings.where: Edge case doc: 'INSERT INTO auth.refresh_token_blacklist (user_id, valid_after=now())' — this table does NOT exist in Supabase GoTrue.
findings.issue: The revocation flow depends on a fictitious auth.refresh_token_blacklist. Real GoTrue revokes via `auth.admin.signOut(user_id, scope='global')` which marks all refresh tokens revoked in auth.refresh_tokens — but the access token is still valid until expiry. The doc conflates the two and may build app logic on a non-existent table.
findings.recommended_fix: Use `supabase.auth.admin.signOut(user_id, scope='global')` only; remove references to refresh_token_blacklist; document that access token revocation is impossible and rely on short TTL + live RLS join on user status (point 3 above).

findings.where: users_self_update policy: `with_check_expr: id = auth.uid() AND role = (SELECT role FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())`
findings.issue: This is supposed to prevent self-role-escalation, but the subquery `SELECT role FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid()` reads the CURRENT (pre-update) role. If the UPDATE itself changes the row, Postgres semantics for `(SELECT ... WHERE id = auth.uid())` inside WITH CHECK evaluates against post-update state in some versions / planner choices, allowing `UPDATE users SET role='admin' WHERE id = auth.uid()` because by the time WITH CHECK runs, role IS 'admin' = 'admin'. Also: this policy lets a user update `tenant_id` to another tenant! Only `role` is locked; tenant_id, status, base_salary, commission_rate are all writable.
findings.recommended_fix: Use a BEFORE UPDATE trigger that compares OLD.role/tenant_id/status/base_salary/commission_rate to NEW and raises if any changed. Restrict the self-update policy to a narrow column whitelist via a view + INSTEAD OF trigger, or split into per-column UPDATE grants (Postgres column-level grants on UPDATE). Add `AND tenant_id = (SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())` to WITH CHECK.

findings.where: JWT claim membership_version, refresh-token denylist using it.
findings.issue: Schema has no membership_version column on public.users (and no tenant_users table at all). The claim therefore cannot be emitted; revocation invalidation depending on it is non-functional.
findings.recommended_fix: Add `membership_version int not null default 1` to public.users (or tenant_users if introduced). Bump in role-change RPC. Reference in middleware to invalidate sessions when JWT version is stale.

findings.where: Edge case doc says past_due = read-only via 'separate WITH CHECK on writes denies'. No such policy shown.
findings.issue: Without the policy, past_due tenants can write freely until tenant_status changes to suspended. Risk: tenant in payment failure continues creating cases/invoices, then claims they should not be billed for that period.
findings.recommended_fix: Add a global guard policy on every tenant table for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE: `WITH CHECK ((auth.jwt()->>'tenant_status') IN ('active','trial'))`. Explicit, not implicit.

findings.where: RLS canonical expression used on every tenant table: `tenant_id = (SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())`
findings.issue: This subquery runs on every row access across every tenant table. Postgres treats it as STABLE only if wrapped — but as written it forces a re-lookup against public.users per policy evaluation per statement. At 500 tenants x 50 users x 10K cases, a list-cases query for an active firm with 200K rows runs the subquery once but the planner often pushes the comparison down such that index access on (tenant_id, created_at DESC) is fine — UNTIL the policy is combined with role checks `(SELECT role FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())` which appears in MANY policies (users_admin_manage, payments_tenant_read, soft-delete visibility). Multiple correlated subqueries against public.users per query × per row evaluation under RLS = repeated index probes. Worse, public.users itself has RLS, causing recursive policy planning that prevents the subquery from being marked STABLE/CACHED across the statement. The rls_principles section even acknowledges the JWT-claim cache alternative but says `Both forms kept equivalent` — they are NOT equivalent at scale.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Make the JWT-claim form (`(auth.jwt() ->> 'tenant_id')::uuid` and `auth.jwt() ->> 'role'`) the CANONICAL form everywhere; the public.users subquery is a fallback only. Populate JWT claims via Supabase auth hook on login + role change. 2) Wrap claim access in a SECURITY DEFINER STABLE function `current_tenant_id()` and `current_user_role()` so the planner caches across the statement. 3) Add `set_config('request.jwt.claims', ...)` discipline at session start. Expect 5-50x speedup on RLS-heavy list queries. 4) Document that the public.users-subquery form is ONLY for fallback / migrations, NEVER hot paths.

findings.where: Addon-gated tables: RLS clause `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tenant_addons WHERE tenant_id = X AND feature_key = 'KEY' AND status='active' AND (expires_at IS NULL OR expires_at > now()))`
findings.issue: This EXISTS runs per row for tables like consulting_hours, weekly_reports, capacity_entries. `now()` is volatile — it forces re-evaluation per row in some plans and disables proof-based partial-index matches. Combined with the tenant_id subquery and role check, a single SELECT against consulting_hours runs 3 correlated subqueries per row evaluation in worst-case plans. Also, the unique partial index `addons_tenant_feature_unique WHERE status='active'` does not account for `expires_at > now()` — a stale 'active' but expired row would still satisfy uniqueness but should not gate access; the policy will allow access while the unique index pretends it's active.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Add a STABLE function `has_addon(feature_key text)` that reads current_tenant_id() and a cached HASH on (tenant_id, feature_key). 2) Run a nightly cron that flips status from 'active' to 'expired' when expires_at passes, then the policy is simply `status='active'` (no now() comparison). 3) Cache addon set in JWT claims as an array `auth.jwt() -> 'addons'` for hot paths — refresh on login + addon purchase webhook. 4) Use `(expires_at IS NULL OR expires_at > now())` ONLY in the cron job, never per-row in RLS.

findings.where: Every tenant-scoped table — particularly cases, documents, memos, schedule_events, tasks, invoices, audit_log, notifications
findings.issue: The indexes_strategy text claims `(tenant_id, created_at DESC)` exists on every tenant table but actual indexes shown for tenants/users/subscriptions/payments only have partial coverage. Specific gaps: (a) notifications channel says realtime fires `recipient_id = auth.uid()` filter — needs `(tenant_id, recipient_id, created_at DESC) WHERE read_at IS NULL` partial index for unread badge counts (hottest query in the app). (b) cases needs `(tenant_id, status, deleted_at) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL AND status IN ('open','in_progress','hearing_scheduled')` for active-cases dashboard. (c) tasks needs `(tenant_id, assignee_id, status) WHERE status != 'done'` for My-Tasks counter. (d) audit_log needs `(tenant_id, entity_type, entity_id, created_at DESC)` for entity-history lookups. (e) schedule_events `(tenant_id, starts_at)` with BRIN only is wrong for selective per-day queries — needs BTREE `(tenant_id, starts_at)` for upcoming-hearings query. (f) memo_history needs `(memo_id, created_at DESC)`. (g) wallet_transactions needs `(tenant_id, user_id, created_at DESC)` for employee wallet view.
findings.recommended_fix: Audit every list/dashboard query in the 38 prototype pages and add a compound matching the (tenant_id, filter_col, sort_col DESC) shape with `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` partial clause where applicable. Concrete must-haves listed above. Build an `pg_stat_statements`-driven index recommendation cycle before launch — at 500 tenants x 100K docs you cannot afford seq scans during peak.

findings.where: audit_log table — `id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY`, no partitioning, write-frequency from triggers on cases/contracts/invoices/salaries/wallets
findings.issue: Projected volume: 500 tenants x 50 users x ~50 audited actions/day = 1.25M rows/day = ~450M rows/year. With JSONB before/after snapshots averaging 2-5KB each, that is 1-2TB/year on one table. Single-table BTREE indexes will balloon, VACUUM windows will lengthen, and tenant-scoped queries (`SELECT * FROM audit_log WHERE tenant_id=X ORDER BY created_at DESC`) will degrade as the table grows. Append-only design is good but missing the partitioning strategy that should accompany it. GDPR hard-delete on a 500M-row monolithic table requires DELETEs that block VACUUM.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Declarative range-partition by `created_at` MONTHLY from day 1. 2) Local indexes on each partition for (tenant_id, created_at DESC) and (tenant_id, entity_type, entity_id). 3) Automated partition creation cron (pg_partman or custom) creates next 3 months ahead. 4) Retention policy: detach partitions older than 7 years (legal compliance), archive to cold storage (S3) via pg_dump. 5) For GDPR hard-delete of a tenant: DELETE WHERE tenant_id=X runs per-partition in parallel and is fast. 6) Consider sub-partitioning by tenant_id hash for the top-10 noisiest tenants if needed. 7) Stop storing full before/after JSONB for `view` actions — only the entity_id is needed.

findings.where: wallets.balance cached column with trigger-based update from wallet_transactions
findings.issue: Design notes state `Balance computed from sum or cached on wallets.balance with trigger`. If cached, every wallet_transaction INSERT triggers UPDATE on wallets row. A single user receiving salary + commissions + reimbursements on payday creates a hot row for that user's wallet; under concurrent inserts (e.g., monthly payroll batch for 50 users x 500 tenants = 25K simultaneous wallet UPDATEs) you get row-lock contention. Worse, the wallet row is FK-referenced from every wallet_transaction insert, causing FK trigger to take SHARE locks. HOT updates may not apply because the wallets row likely has indexes.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Prefer sum-on-read with a covering index on wallet_transactions(tenant_id, user_id, created_at) INCLUDE (amount, tx_type) and materialize a daily snapshot table `wallet_daily_balances(tenant_id, user_id, date, opening, closing)` updated by cron. Query becomes `latest_snapshot + sum_since_snapshot`. 2) If cached balance is required (for UI), use an event-sourced async worker rather than synchronous trigger — write transactions append-only, recompute balance every 5s via a background job per dirty wallet. 3) Never use SELECT FOR UPDATE on the wallet row from API code — use advisory locks scoped to user_id. 4) Document the contention model in the wallet module README.

findings.where: notifications channel + user:{user_id}:inbox + tenant:{tenant_id}:notifications
findings.issue: Cost-concerns acknowledges the 500-concurrent-conn cap is hit at 5-15 active tenants, but the design still subscribes EVERY user to BOTH tenant-wide notifications AND user inbox. At 50 users x 500 tenants = 25K concurrent connections target — 50x over the limit before any of the per-case/per-memo dynamic channels open. Also, realtime fan-out runs RLS per recipient per row; with the current `(SELECT tenant_id FROM public.users WHERE id = auth.uid())` policy, every NOTIFY does a join. The mentions and task_assignments tables on the user:{user_id}:inbox channel will be high-frequency at scale. No mention of using Supabase Realtime Broadcast vs postgres_changes for the bell icon — postgres_changes is wasteful here because it carries the full row payload per event.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Bell-icon unread counter: switch to a server-side counter cache in Redis/Upstash, only push an increment broadcast (not a row payload). 2) Consolidate `tenant:{tenant_id}:notifications` AND `user:{user_id}:inbox` into a single user-private channel — tenant-wide announcements get inserted as per-user rows OR delivered via broadcast (not DB). 3) Realtime RLS MUST use the JWT-claim form (not the public.users subquery) for fan-out speed. 4) Concrete plan needed for the 500-conn ceiling: connection pooling via custom WebSocket gateway, OR commit to upgrading to Supabase Team/Enterprise tier on day 1 of public launch. 5) Implement the circuit-breaker mentioned in cost_concerns as actual code, not a note. 6) Drop postgres_changes on memo_workflow_status and case_documents — these are low-frequency, poll on tab focus instead.

findings.where: RLS principle: `SELECT policies include AND deleted_at IS NULL except for admin/auditor/general_manager`
findings.issue: Partial indexes `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` are listed as the strategy, but two issues: (1) the partial filter inside RLS using `deleted_at IS NULL` combined with `role NOT IN ('admin','auditor','general_manager')` produces a conditional that the planner cannot always match to a partial index — depends on which role the runtime resolves to. The role is determined by another correlated subquery, making the planner unable to choose at plan time. (2) The cron `nightly purge past 30 days` will DELETE potentially huge batches; bulk DELETE on heavily indexed tables (cases, documents, memos) without batching will bloat indexes and stall VACUUM. (3) Cascading FKs on soft-deleted rows: when a case is soft-deleted, do related case_team, case_timeline rows also get filtered? Not specified — risk of orphan visibility in case-detail subqueries.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Use TWO separate policies per table — `<table>_active_select` with `deleted_at IS NULL` for all roles, and `<table>_admin_full_select` with no deleted_at filter for admin/auditor — Postgres will OR them and the planner picks the partial index. 2) Nightly purge: batched DELETE in 5K-row chunks with sleep, run during quiet hours, follow with REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on high-write tables monthly. 3) Document the cascade rule: child rows of soft-deleted parents are filtered at view/RLS level via the parent's deleted_at, OR child rows get their own deleted_at set transactionally with the parent.

findings.where: GIN indexes with `to_tsvector('arabic', col)` on cases.title_ar, memos.body_ar, regulations.text_ar, templates.body_ar, precedents.text_ar
findings.issue: Postgres ships an `arabic` text search configuration but it is rudimentary — uses a simple stemmer that does NOT handle: (a) the definite article `ال` prefix, (b) diacritics (تشكيل) — searches for `محكمة` won't match `مَحْكَمَة`, (c) hamza forms (أ/إ/ا collapsing), (d) tah-marbuta vs ha normalization (ة/ه), (e) yeh forms (ي/ى). At 500 tenants with thousands of cases and memos in Arabic, recall will be poor and partners will complain. Also, regulations.text_ar can be very long (full law texts) — to_tsvector on multi-MB strings is slow and the GIN index grows quickly.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Custom text search config: pre-normalize text via a `unaccent`-style function PLUS Arabic-specific normalization (strip diacritics, collapse hamza forms, normalize tah-marbuta, normalize yeh, strip tatweel). Store a generated column `search_doc tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (arabic_normalize_to_tsvector(coalesce(title_ar,'') || ' ' || coalesce(description_ar,''))) STORED` and GIN-index that. 2) Evaluate `pg_trgm` GIN as a complementary index for fuzzy/substring matching of Arabic proper nouns (client names, opponent names) — works well even without language config. 3) For regulations.text_ar (potentially massive), consider chunked indexing (paragraph-level rows) rather than indexing whole-document tsvectors. 4) Long-term: if quality matters, externalize to OpenSearch/Meilisearch with proper Arabic analyzers — Postgres FTS is a tactical solution, not a strategic one. 5) Benchmark recall on a sample corpus before launch — if recall < 80%, externalize.

findings.where: address (tenants.address, presumably clients.address), settings, metadata, raw_payload, changes, payload columns
findings.issue: Schema mostly does this right — design_notes explicitly lists JSONB usage discipline. But: (1) `tenants.settings` holds locale + timezone + currency — these will be read on every request (for date formatting, currency display) yet require a JSONB extract operator per access. Locale + timezone should be top-level columns. (2) `audit_log.changes` JSONB without a GIN index will be searched by auditors (`who changed status to lost?`) — at 450M rows, full scans will be impossible; queries will time out. (3) `notifications.payload` will be queried by type even though notification_type column exists — risk of teams putting business data inside payload. (4) `payments.raw_payload` is unbounded webhook payloads; over 500 tenants x many payments/yr this single column may dominate the table's TOAST storage and slow seq scans.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Promote tenants.settings hot keys (locale, timezone, currency, fiscal_year_start) to top-level columns. 2) Add `GIN (changes jsonb_path_ops)` on audit_log per-partition for auditor queries OR extract the most-queried keys (e.g., new_status, old_status) into generated columns. 3) Hard rule documented: payments.raw_payload retained for 90 days then nulled (keep moyasar_payment_id, status, amount). 4) Lint at API layer: forbid storing structured business data in `metadata` JSONB without schema review.

findings.where: Case list views, memo workflow status, dashboard widgets
findings.issue: Schema is normalized which is correct, but standard PostgREST/Supabase client usage tends to cause N+1 when fetching cases with assignees, clients, and latest timeline event. With 10K cases per mid-tier tenant and a dashboard showing 50 cases with 5 related entities each = 250 client calls per page render unless caller uses `select=*,client:clients(*),assignees:users(*)` embedding. Memo workflow page risks N+1 when fetching memo + history + approvals + comments + author. Wallet ledger view risks N+1 when fetching transactions + counterparty user info per row.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Define a set of denormalized read-models / Postgres VIEWs for the top-10 hot dashboards (case_list_v, memo_detail_v, my_wallet_v, my_tasks_v). 2) Document mandatory PostgREST embedding syntax for each list endpoint in the frontend API client. 3) Consider materialized views refreshed every 5 minutes for analytics-style queries (Reports/Intel module — 'cases won this quarter by lawyer') rather than live aggregations. 4) Add `pg_stat_statements` monitoring and alert on any query running >100x/min — likely N+1 culprits.

findings.where: subscriptions: `subscriptions_tenant_active_unique columns=[tenant_id] WHERE status IN ('trialing','active','past_due')`
findings.issue: This partial unique index correctly enforces one active subscription per tenant. BUT: a tenant moving from `past_due` -> `cancelled` then immediately starting a new `trialing` plan would be allowed — fine. However the index ALSO must consider that webhook race conditions from Moyasar can momentarily insert a duplicate `pending`/`initiated` row before the existing one is marked cancelled. Without an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT path or explicit advisory lock around webhook handling, duplicate active subscriptions can sneak through during webhook retries. Similar concern on `payments_moyasar_payment_unique` — good that it exists, but webhook idempotency must use it via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING/UPDATE.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Webhook handler must run within a transaction taking advisory lock `pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('moyasar:'||tenant_id))` before mutating subscriptions. 2) All payment inserts use `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (moyasar_payment_id) DO UPDATE SET status=...` for idempotency. 3) Add a separate `moyasar_webhook_events` raw inbox table with primary key on event_id for replay-safe processing.

findings.where: Per-case channels `tenant:{tenant_id}:case:{case_id}` claim 'RLS double-checks tenant_id AND case access (case_team membership or role >= partner)'
findings.issue: Realtime evaluates RLS on EVERY change event for EVERY subscriber. The case_team membership check requires a join — at a tenant with 50 users and 5K active cases, every case_timeline_events INSERT triggers per-subscriber RLS that joins case_team. With 20 viewers on a hot case and 10 inserts/min, that's 200 RLS evaluations/min just for one case. Across the firm during business hours this is sustained CPU on the realtime worker.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Denormalize: add `case_visible_to uuid[]` on case_timeline_events / case_documents pre-computed at INSERT time, RLS becomes `auth.uid() = ANY(case_visible_to)` — index-friendly, no join. 2) OR enforce that only `role >= partner` subscribes to the per-case channel via the realtime subscribe-side filter, and lawyers/secretary use polling for case detail (acceptable since they're already in the page). 3) Mark the helper `can_access_case(case_id uuid)` SECURITY DEFINER STABLE PARALLEL SAFE so realtime can cache.

findings.where: Multiple crons referenced: nightly soft-delete purge, trial-to-past_due flip, addon expiry flip, audit-export 30-day purge
findings.issue: Design notes mention several crons but no single source of truth for scheduling, retry policy, or visibility. At 500 tenants the nightly soft-delete purge across cases/documents/memos/timeline/history could run for hours and conflict with backups + autovacuum. The trial-expiry flip needs to run frequently (hourly?) to avoid users having a paid feature available for hours after trial end — currently undefined cadence.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Use pg_cron or Supabase Scheduled Functions with a documented registry of all jobs: name, cadence, max runtime, last_run, last_status. 2) Soft-delete purge: batched 1K rows per iteration with LIMIT + LOCK SKIP LOCKED, can run continuously. 3) Trial flip: every 15 minutes. 4) Addon expiry flip: every 15 minutes. 5) Audit export purge: daily off-peak. 6) Add alerting if any cron skips a run or exceeds max runtime.

findings.where: Not addressed in schema — Postgres connection model under 500 tenants
findings.issue: Schema is silent on connection management. Supabase ships PgBouncer/Supavisor but with 500 tenants of users each making PostgREST + direct-DB queries + edge function calls, raw connection count will pressure the cluster. Long-lived realtime connections do NOT use the pooler but background analytics queries do — mis-configuration here will surface as random 503s.
findings.recommended_fix: 1) Document that all backend (server-side) code MUST use the transaction-pooled URL (Supavisor port). 2) Long-running queries (reports/intel) must explicitly use the session-pooled or direct connection with a statement_timeout cap. 3) Add per-tenant connection accounting if any tenant generates >5% of total query load — they get a separate read replica eventually. 4) Set `statement_timeout=30s` and `idle_in_transaction_session_timeout=60s` cluster-wide.

findings.where: Schema vs Migrations divergence
findings.issue: The SCHEMA payload and MIGRATIONS payload disagree on fundamental naming. SCHEMA uses subscription_tier values 'tier_49','tier_199','tier_499','tier_1999' while migrations use 'base_49','pro_199','business_499','enterprise_1999'. SCHEMA uses case_status values like 'draft','open','in_progress','hearing_scheduled','awaiting_judgment','won','lost' while migrations use 'intake','open','on_hold','in_court','judgement_pending','closed_won','closed_lost'. SCHEMA puts tables in public schema; migrations split into app/platform/audit schemas. SCHEMA helper uses auth.uid(); migrations use app.current_user_id(). This is a generator/spec drift that will produce two incompatible codebases.
findings.recommended_fix: Pick one source of truth before writing any SQL. Recommend the migrations' app/platform/audit schema split (cleaner boundary, portable away from Supabase) but with SCHEMA's RLS expressions (auth.uid() is one less indirection). Reconcile every enum value list and re-emit a single canonical doc; reject the orchestrator's parallel generation until both align.

findings.where: enums: case_status, request_status, memo_status, subscription_status
findings.issue: Mixed tense and verb forms: case_status mixes participles ('archived','settled') with present-tense ('hearing_scheduled','awaiting_judgment') and adjectives ('open'). subscription_status has 'trialing' (gerund) alongside 'active','expired' (adjectives). request_status mixes 'pending','in_review','approved'. Inconsistency makes UI label maps and i18n keys awkward.
findings.recommended_fix: Adopt one convention: past-participle/adjective for terminal states ('archived','closed','approved','expired') and present-progressive for in-flight states ('trialing','reviewing','hearing_scheduled'). Document the convention in a CONTRIBUTING.md so future enums comply. Add a 'enum value style guide' comment at top of 002_enums.sql.

findings.where: users.full_name_ar / name_ar / title_ar columns
findings.issue: Bilingual columns use inconsistent suffixes: tenants uses name_ar/name_en, users uses full_name_ar/full_name_en, cases uses title_ar (no _en pair shown), clients uses name_ar/name_en. The mix of name vs full_name vs title is fine semantically but TypeScript codegen will produce awkward unions and the i18n helper has to special-case each.
findings.recommended_fix: Standardize the bilingual suffix pattern: every translatable user-visible string column is <semantic>_ar and <semantic>_en, always nullable on _en, always NOT NULL on _ar. Add a check constraint that length(trim(name_ar)) > 0. Provide a SQL view 'app.localized_<table>' that returns a single 'display_name' column based on a session GUC so TS codegen sees one column.

findings.where: users.commission_rate numeric(5,4)
findings.issue: A 0..1 rate stored as numeric(5,4) is a frequent source of '100x bugs' (someone inserts 15 meaning 15%). No check constraint shown. Also the column lives on users which conflates identity with role-payout config; BD/marketer may have different rates per campaign or per case-type.
findings.recommended_fix: Add CHECK (commission_rate >= 0 AND commission_rate <= 1). Better: move to a separate app.commission_schemes table (user_id, scope, rate, effective_from, effective_to) so historical commissions stay correct when the rate changes. Rename to commission_fraction or commission_rate_0_to_1 to make the unit obvious to anyone scanning the column.

findings.where: tenants.address jsonb, tenants.settings jsonb
findings.issue: address jsonb has no documented shape; settings jsonb has only a hint ('Locale, timezone, currency, branding tokens'). Frontend devs and codegen will have to guess. JSONB is appropriate but the shape should be enforced.
findings.recommended_fix: Add a CHECK constraint with jsonb_typeof or a small jsonb_matches_schema function call (pg_jsonschema extension), or split known fields into normal columns: locale, timezone, currency, default_invoice_prefix. Keep settings jsonb only for truly unbounded keys. Publish a TypeScript type 'TenantSettings' next to the migration and reference it in a column COMMENT.

findings.where: payments.raw_payload, webhook_events.payload
findings.issue: Both store the same Moyasar event but raw_payload lives on payments AND webhook_events keeps payload. Risk of divergence and double storage. Unclear which is canonical for replay/idempotency.
findings.recommended_fix: Make webhook_events the single canonical store of raw payloads (append-only, partitioned by month for retention). payments.raw_payload becomes a FK reference webhook_event_id uuid plus a generated/derived snapshot column if needed. Document: 'replay always reads webhook_events; payments is the projection.'

findings.where: No atomic operations defined for hot paths
findings.issue: Several flows MUST be atomic but the schema relies on the client to coordinate: (a) tenant signup (insert tenant + subscription + admin user + wallet) -- partial failure leaves orphaned tenants; (b) Moyasar webhook (upsert webhook_event, update subscription, create payment, credit wallet) -- needs SERIALIZABLE; (c) case assignment change (insert case_assignments, write case_timeline, send notification); (d) invoice mark_paid (update invoice, insert wallet_transaction for commissions, write audit). Without RPCs, every junior dev re-implements these and breaks the audit invariant.
findings.recommended_fix: Add SECURITY DEFINER PL/pgSQL functions in an api schema: api.signup_tenant(p_tenant jsonb, p_owner jsonb) RETURNS uuid, api.handle_moyasar_event(p_event jsonb) RETURNS void, api.assign_case(p_case_id uuid, p_user_id uuid, p_role text) RETURNS void, api.mark_invoice_paid(p_invoice_id uuid, p_amount numeric) RETURNS void. Each function: validates tenant via app.current_tenant_id(), wraps in single transaction, writes audit_log row, returns typed result. Expose only api.* via PostgREST; revoke direct table grants on the underlying mutations.

findings.where: Feature-flag check is inline EXISTS in every gated policy
findings.issue: RLS policies on addon-gated tables embed EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM tenant_addons ...) literally. As more gated tables appear, the expression duplicates, drifts, and can't be tweaked centrally (e.g., adding 'grace period' or 'is_in_trial' override).
findings.recommended_fix: Define app.has_feature(p_feature_key text) RETURNS boolean STABLE SECURITY DEFINER (cached per statement via STABLE). Every addon-gated policy becomes 'tenant_id = app.current_tenant_id() AND app.has_feature('consulting_hours')'. Single place to add trial-grace, sub-status check, expiry tolerance. Also add app.require_feature(text) RETURNS void for RPC use that RAISES a typed error code (PGRST -> 402 Payment Required) for explicit UX cases.

findings.where: Addon empty-result vs error tradeoff
findings.issue: 'Empty result, not error' satisfies the non-negotiable for RLS but leaves the frontend unable to distinguish 'no data' from 'no entitlement' -- they will repeatedly build their own check by querying tenant_addons themselves, defeating cache strategies.
findings.recommended_fix: Expose a read-only RPC api.my_features() RETURNS table(feature_key text, active boolean, expires_at timestamptz) so the SPA fetches the entitlement set once per session, gates UI elements client-side, and the RLS still acts as the hard backstop. Add a column-level RLS-aware view app.tenant_entitlements that the frontend can subscribe to via Supabase realtime.

findings.where: Migration file list has implicit cross-deps
findings.issue: 008_cases_core.sql depends_on 007_clients_contacts.sql AND 006_lookup_saudi.sql, but 006 doesn't depend on 005, so courts/regions seed before users exists -- fine, but the dependency graph is not a straight chain. Also: RLS policy definitions are not shown in the migration sequence (when does each table get its RLS?). If RLS is enabled in a later file (e.g., 099_rls.sql) the window between table creation and policy creation is a 'world-readable' liability if anyone runs migrations against a populated db.
findings.recommended_fix: Move 'ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;' to the SAME file that creates the table, immediately after table creation, BEFORE any GRANT or seed. Add policies in the same file. Use a single migration_meta.yaml that lists files with explicit DAG and a CI lint that rejects PRs that create a table without RLS enable in the same file. Rename files to use a 3-segment scheme: 010_platform_tenants.sql, 011_platform_billing.sql, 020_app_identity.sql -- the gap pattern keeps insertion room and the prefix groups by domain.

findings.where: Seed data (Saudi regions, courts, plans, addons) timing
findings.issue: 006_lookup_saudi.sql 'seeds 13 Saudi regions here' inside the schema migration -- mixes DDL with reference data. Plans and addons (priced data) must also be seeded but aren't called out. Devs running prod migrations may inadvertently re-seed.
findings.recommended_fix: Split into 006_lookup_schema.sql (DDL only) and 006a_lookup_data.sql (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING with stable UUIDs). Mark data files idempotent. For plans/addons add 004a_billing_seeds.sql. Provide a make target 'make seeds-prod' separate from 'make migrate' so reference data is a deliberate step.

findings.where: Multi-schema split (app/platform/audit) vs Supabase TypeScript generator
findings.issue: supabase gen types typescript --schema public is the default and easiest path. Splitting into app/platform/audit is correct from a permissions standpoint but every codegen run needs --schema app,platform,audit and produces a Database type with nested namespaces that don't auto-import cleanly in IDE. New devs hit 'cannot find table' errors when they forget the schema name in .from('cases').
findings.recommended_fix: Either: (a) keep everything in public but use grants + RLS for the boundary -- accept the namespacing loss for DX; or (b) keep multi-schema but export PUBLIC views (CREATE VIEW public.cases AS SELECT * FROM app.cases) with security_invoker=true so .from('cases') still works and codegen sees them. Document the pattern in CONTRIBUTING and add a CI check that every app.* table has a matching public view.

findings.where: Enum naming case_type values like 'ip' (intellectual property)
findings.issue: 'ip' as enum value becomes type CaseType = 'ip' | 'civil' | ... in TS; reading code at the call site is opaque. Same for 'sca','moj' in regulation_jurisdiction.
findings.recommended_fix: Use full snake_case identifiers: 'intellectual_property', 'saudi_central_bank' or 'capital_market_authority', 'ministry_of_justice'. Adds clarity in TS without hurting SQL. Provide a SQL CASE expression in views for short display codes if needed.

findings.where: deleted_at present on tenants, users, clients, cases, documents, etc. -- but missing on payments, wallet_transactions, audit_log (correctly), and also on subscriptions, tenant_addons
findings.issue: Some user-facing tables have deleted_at, others don't, and the rules are not documented. tenant_addons being cancelled is a status change ('cancelled') NOT a deleted_at -- good -- but subscriptions has cancelled_at and no deleted_at, while users uses deleted_at. The frontend filter pattern (.is('deleted_at', null)) will silently fail to filter on subscriptions because the column doesn't exist (Supabase returns empty if column missing).
findings.recommended_fix: Document the soft-delete contract: 'user-facing entity tables = deleted_at; billing/state tables = status enum + cancelled_at; append-only = neither.' Add the contract as a comment in 002_enums.sql header. Create a SQL view app.active_<table> for every soft-deletable table that pre-applies WHERE deleted_at IS NULL so frontend queries are uniform. Lint: any table with deleted_at MUST have partial index WHERE deleted_at IS NULL.

findings.where: 30-day purge cron
findings.issue: Design says 'nightly cron purges rows past 30 days for hard delete' but the migration list has no cron job, no pg_cron extension, no app.scheduled_jobs registry. The non-negotiable will rot.
findings.recommended_fix: Add 001_extensions.sql: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_cron. Add 099_cron_jobs.sql that registers SELECT cron.schedule('purge-soft-deleted-30d', '0 3 * * *', $$ CALL app.purge_soft_deleted(30) $$). Implement app.purge_soft_deleted(p_days int) procedure that iterates over a metadata table app.soft_deletable_tables (name text PK, retention_days int) and executes DELETE with cascade. Visible, testable, configurable per table.

findings.where: Postgres ENUM types vs lookup tables
findings.issue: All status fields are Postgres ENUMs. ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE cannot run inside a transaction and cannot be removed -- every new status requires a one-off out-of-tx migration. For long-lived enums like case_status (Saudi court system may add new states), this becomes painful. Also blocks per-tenant customisation entirely (firm wants 'awaiting_translation' state -- impossible).
findings.recommended_fix: Keep ENUM for truly closed sets (user_role, payment_status, billing_cycle). Convert evolving lifecycle enums (case_status, request_status, lead_status, task_status) to small reference tables: app.case_statuses(code text PK, name_ar, name_en, is_terminal bool, sort_order int, is_system bool). Columns become text with FK. Allows per-tenant additions via where is_system or via separate app.tenant_case_statuses(tenant_id, code, ...). Trade-off: lose Postgres enum compactness, gain flexibility.

findings.where: Migrations file 002 plan_tier enum 'base_49' vs SCHEMA 'tier_49'
findings.issue: Price-coded enum values ('tier_49','pro_199') bind the schema to a price point. The day pricing changes from 199 SAR to 249 SAR, the enum value is misleading forever.
findings.recommended_fix: Use abstract tier names: trial, starter, pro, business, enterprise. Store the actual price on subscription_plans.monthly_price_sar (already there). Decouples schema from marketing decisions.

findings.where: ID column naming and FK convention
findings.issue: Migrations use 'tenant_id NOT NULL fk platform.tenants' style. SCHEMA shows column 'tenant_id uuid'. Naming is consistent (snake_case, _id suffix). However plural-vs-singular mismatch: junction table case_clients(case_id, client_id) is fine, but case_assignments has user_id (the assignee) -- not assigned_user_id or assignee_id -- which is ambiguous when combined with created_by/assigned_by. Same pattern in tasks (assignee_user_id vs user_id is unclear).
findings.recommended_fix: Adopt FK column convention: '<role>_user_id' when the relationship has a semantic name (assignee_user_id, assigned_by_user_id, created_by_user_id, lead_lawyer_user_id). Use bare 'user_id' only when the row IS the user's row (notifications, wallets). Rename users.id remains 'id'. Document in the column-naming style guide section.

findings.where: Search across cases/memos/regulations in Arabic
findings.issue: GIN indexes are defined on individual columns (cases.title_ar, memos.body_ar, regulations.text_ar) but no unified RPC for the global search bar. Frontend will issue three parallel queries, sort client-side, lose ranking quality.
findings.recommended_fix: Add api.search_all(p_query text, p_limit int default 20) RETURNS table(entity_type text, entity_id uuid, title text, snippet text, rank real) that uses websearch_to_tsquery('arabic', p_query) and UNION ALLs the indexed tables with ts_rank. Apply tenant_id = app.current_tenant_id() inside. Single round-trip, server-side ranking.

findings.where: tenant_addons.feature_key is free-form text
findings.issue: feature_key is text with example values in a comment. Typos in policy expressions ('intel_reports' vs 'intel_report') will silently disable the gate.
findings.recommended_fix: Define enum app.feature_key OR a reference table app.addon_catalog(feature_key text PK, name_ar, name_en, price_sar). Add FK from tenant_addons.feature_key. Then policy literals are validated at migration time -- a misspelt key in a policy will fail to add the policy if you also add a deferred check, or at minimum will fail the addon insert.
